title: Explorer Desktop Sweep
goal: Explore the main desktop experience after acceptance passes, while avoiding tunnel vision around only the targeted control under test.
intent: Review the explorer like an impatient user deciding what to solve next, while also checking whether the surrounding product still feels trustworthy and coherent.
reviewQuestions: Would the page help a user choose a useful next problem without excess scanning?
Do the content surfaces feel like teaching support instead of database spillover?
After each interaction, is the active state obvious and is the rest of the workspace still stable?
reminders: After each targeted action, scan the surrounding layout, not just the primary widget.
Check for weak active state, stale content, clipping, scroll instability, inconsistent spacing, and accidental visual regressions nearby.
Compare what changed against what should have stayed stable.
Notice copy tone, metadata noise, and whether the page still reads like a coherent tool.
baseline: Run the stable acceptance suite first so exploratory work starts from a known-functional baseline.
Keep screenshots reproducible with session-scoped names and ordered step numbers.
Write observations as evidence, not vibes.

steps.title: Landing workspace
steps.intent: Judge the first-load workspace as a decision tool, not as a checklist of components.
steps.focus: Assess the initial desktop hierarchy and whether the workspace relationship feels stable.
lookAround: Does the sidebar feel proportional to the main workspace?
Does the collapsed roadmap stay secondary without disappearing conceptually?
Does the selected row feel obviously connected to the detail pane?
reviewQuestions: Would a new user immediately understand how to start from this state?
steps.observation: Initial workspace renders with a stable catalog/detail split and a visible recommendation layer.

steps.title: Select Decode Ways
steps.intent: Verify that deliberate selection changes feel obvious and worth reading.
steps.focus: Inspect active-state clarity, detail-panel update quality, and row-to-detail linkage.
lookAround: Do nearby rows still feel scannable after selection?
Does the detail pane remain readable instead of becoming box-noise?
Do repo cards and action links stay balanced below the main recommendation copy?
reviewQuestions: Does the selected row look unmistakably active, not merely clicked?
steps.observation: Selected-row highlighting remains readable and the detail pane updates correctly for Decode Ways.

steps.title: Apply BFS quick view
steps.intent: Test whether a narrower view still feels curated instead of accidental.
steps.focus: Check whether a narrowed slice still feels coherent rather than arbitrarily filtered.
lookAround: Does the active-filter summary stay legible?
Does the recommendation surface adapt cleanly to the filtered slice?
Does the catalog still feel grouped rather than starved?
reviewQuestions: Does this filtered state still help the user choose rather than simply reduce the count?
steps.observation: The BFS quick view narrows the catalog without collapsing the recommendation layer into noise.

steps.title: Search down to a single result
steps.intent: Check how the workspace behaves when the catalog is almost empty.
steps.focus: Inspect empty-space handling and narrow-state stability.
lookAround: Does the page look broken when the catalog gets very short?
Do list count, selected state, and detail content remain internally consistent?
Does the surrounding chrome still feel intentional with only one visible row?
reviewQuestions: Does the single-result state still feel like a tool, not a layout failure?
steps.observation: Single-result search state stays stable; list count and detail remain consistent.

steps.title: Select from roadmap
steps.intent: Verify that the roadmap acts like a working navigation surface.
steps.focus: Check whether the roadmap behaves like an operational navigation device instead of a disconnected diagram.
lookAround: Does opening the roadmap disturb the rest of the page too much?
Does selection flow cleanly back into the detail pane?
Do roadmap labels and node density still look readable at desktop width?
reviewQuestions: Does this interaction make the roadmap feel essential enough to keep?
steps.observation: Roadmap selection correctly feeds back into the detail pane and keeps the explorer state coherent.

steps.title: Open a topic guide
steps.intent: Test whether the learning layer feels first-class, not bolted on.
steps.focus: Check whether topic pages feel like a first-class companion surface rather than a detached appendix.
lookAround: Does the transition from explorer to topic page preserve trust?
Does the topic page keep the same design language?
Would a user understand how to get back and continue work?
reviewQuestions: Do the topic pages now feel worth opening even if the user is not solving immediately?
steps.observation: Topic-guide navigation works and preserves the same design language closely enough for a companion surface.
